does: This paper identifies and systematically characterizes Multi-Concept Compositional Unsafety (MCCU), a vulnerability in text-to-image models where individually safe concepts combine to generate harmful outputs. The authors develop TwoHamsters, a large-scale evaluation framework with 20,000 prompts, 51 curated concept pairs, and six risk categories to measure this threat across 13 T2I models in a black-box setting. Key findings reveal a fundamental tension between instruction-following fidelity and safety compliance: models like FLUX.1 that excel at faithful prompt adherence (99.35% unsafe alignment rate) simultaneously fail to defend against compositional risks (1.57% defense rate), demonstrating that conventional safety mechanisms inadequately address this emergent vulnerability class.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about T2I model safety and jailbreaking vulnerabilities, extending beyond adversarial prompt injection attacks and access-based exploits that dominated prior research. It responds to the observation that modern T2I models increasingly prioritize instruction-following as a primary objective, connecting to broader research on alignment and safety-utility tradeoffs in generative models. The paper relates to prior work on compositional understanding in vision-language models and adversarial robustness, while addressing a gap in safety evaluation frameworks that overlook how benign concepts interact. It complements research on prompt-based safety testing and systematic red-teaming methodologies by introducing a novel threat taxonomy focused on compositional failure modes rather than explicit disallowed content.
enables: This research opens directions for developing safety mechanisms that account for compositional interactions without sacrificing instruction-following capability. It motivates future work on compositional safety filters, concept-interaction detection systems, and improved training approaches that can distinguish harmful from benign concept combinations. The TwoHamsters framework provides infrastructure for benchmarking defenses and understanding whether generalization of safety constraints to unseen compositions is achievable. The findings also enable research into the fundamental nature of the instruction-following versus safety tradeoff, potentially leading to novel architectural or training approaches that preserve both capabilities. Finally, this work could inspire similar compositional vulnerability studies in other generative domains and informs policy discussions around responsible deployment of increasingly capable generative models.
